concepts.lesson: Health is not only the absence of disease but a complete state of physical, mental, and social well-being. A healthy person can perform daily activities efficiently, maintain positive relationships, and cope with challenges. According to the World Health Organization, true health involves balance in body, mind, and social life.
concepts.real_example: A student who eats nutritious food, exercises regularly, feels happy, and maintains good friendships is considered healthy.
key_points: Health includes physical, mental, and social well-being

concepts.lesson: Maintaining a healthy lifestyle involves eating a balanced diet, exercising regularly, getting enough sleep, and maintaining personal hygiene. Avoiding junk food, excessive screen time, and harmful substances such as tobacco and alcohol also contributes to good health.
concepts.real_example: Eating fruits and vegetables, exercising daily, and getting proper sleep help maintain a healthy body.

concepts.lesson: The environment in which we live greatly affects our health. Clean surroundings, pure air, and safe drinking water reduce the risk of diseases. Pollution, contaminated water, and unhygienic surroundings can increase the spread of infections and respiratory problems.
concepts.real_example: People living near polluted areas may develop breathing problems such as asthma.
key_points: Air and water pollution harm health

concepts.lesson: Symptoms are the feelings or experiences of illness reported by a patient, such as pain or tiredness. Signs are observable or measurable indicators of illness, such as fever or swelling. Both symptoms and signs help doctors diagnose diseases.
concepts.real_example: Feeling tired is a symptom, while high body temperature during fever is a sign.
key_points: Symptoms are felt by the patient

concepts.lesson: Communicable diseases are illnesses caused by pathogens such as bacteria, viruses, fungi, or worms. These diseases can spread from one person to another through air, water, food, direct contact, or vectors like mosquitoes.
concepts.real_example: Malaria spreads through mosquito bites, and influenza spreads through air droplets.
key_points: Transmission through air, water, food, or insects

concepts.lesson: Non-communicable diseases are not caused by pathogens and do not spread from one person to another. They often result from lifestyle factors such as poor diet, lack of exercise, or environmental influences. Many of these diseases are chronic and require long-term management.
concepts.real_example: Diabetes, cancer, and asthma are examples of non-communicable diseases.

concepts.lesson: Immunity is the body’s natural ability to fight against disease-causing organisms. Vaccines strengthen immunity by exposing the body to weakened or harmless forms of pathogens so that the immune system learns to recognize and fight them effectively.
concepts.real_example: Polio vaccines protect children from polio infection.
key_points: Immunity protects the body from pathogens

concepts.lesson: Antibiotics are medicines used to treat bacterial infections. They work by killing bacteria or preventing them from multiplying. However, antibiotics are not effective against viruses. Overuse or misuse of antibiotics can lead to antibiotic resistance, making bacteria harder to treat.
concepts.real_example: Penicillin is one of the earliest antibiotics used to treat bacterial infections.
